caveats: a: OEWS combines indoor theaters, drive-ins, and other motion-picture exhibition at 512130.
a: Occupation shares are employment-based and require an approximate wage and task mapping.
a: Large-chain technology described by Marcus may be more advanced than lower-middle-market independent theaters.
rho: Observed digitization is not evidence that AI has delivered equivalent labor removal.
rho: Marcus is larger and better capitalized than the screened LMM population.
rho: Some remaining exposed tasks occur only during peaks, limiting removable staffing.
e: No public source measures eligibility within the supplied LMM-band firm count.
e: The supplied n uses a general Information Services EBITDA margin that may not fit film-rental, concession, and lease economics.
e: A financially active location can be part of a larger chain rather than a separately transferable firm.
s5: BizBuySell's theater benchmark includes nightclubs, live-performance venues, and smaller businesses outside the supplied EBITDA band.
s5: The seller survey has no exact-industry denominator and measures intentions, not completed transfers.
s5: Theater closures and property dispositions do not qualify when the operating service does not survive.
q: Price increases during inflation do not directly measure retention of AI-enabled savings.
q: Film-rental terms vary by title and may capture part of admissions economics.
q: Independent theaters may reinvest savings to remain competitive rather than retain them as cash flow.
d5: Box office is nominal revenue, not constant-price quantity.
d5: Public-chain attendance and trade-group surveys may not represent LMM independent theaters.
d5: Demand depends unusually heavily on distributor release schedules and a small number of titles.
o: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required share.
o: The trade association has an advocacy interest in emphasizing experiential durability.
o: Self-service changes labor intensity without necessarily removing the theater operator.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Consumers pay per admission or through memberships and also buy concessions and premium experiences; operators may earn auditorium-rental, advertising, ticket-fee, loyalty, gift-card, merchandise, and alternate-content revenue. Film rental is commonly linked to admissions revenue, while venue labor, occupancy, utilities, and most technology costs remain with the exhibitor.
lens.excluded_activities: Drive-in theaters, film production and distribution, streaming services, captive screening rooms, dormant venues, non-operating real-estate owners, and film festivals or itinerant exhibitors without a recurring operating organization.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market operators of indoor motion-picture theaters that repeatedly sell admissions and related concessions, food and beverage, advertising, loyalty, rental, and event services to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for the combined 512130 motion-picture and video exhibition industry, not exact 512131 or task hours. It reports 58.38% of employment in personal-care/service occupations, chiefly ushers and ticket takers; 24.75% in food preparation and serving; 6.58% in sales; 5.27% in management; and 1.04% in office administration. I approximate wage weighting from the published group employment and mean wages, assign higher remaining exposure to scheduling, forecasting, marketing, customer support, bookkeeping, pricing support, and cashiering, and much lower exposure to food preparation, cleaning, security, maintenance, crowd service, and physical venue operation. Marcus already uses online ordering, self-service ticketing and concession systems, so I discount tasks already automated. Combining drive-ins with indoor theaters likely modestly overstates manual outdoor operations and understates indoor chain administration; occupation groups still do not reveal task hours.
caveats: OEWS combines indoor theaters, drive-ins, and other motion-picture exhibition at 512130.
Occupation shares are employment-based and require an approximate wage and task mapping.
Large-chain technology described by Marcus may be more advanced than lower-middle-market independent theaters.
a.change_evidence: An exact-512131 wage-weighted time study by workflow and theater size, separating current kiosk, mobile-ordering, projection, scheduling, and POS automation from remaining hours, plus audited AI task-success and labor-hour outcomes, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Most theater employment is physical guest service, food service, cleaning, supervision, and facility operation. AI can reduce some management, scheduling, marketing, customer-service, reconciliation, and sales-support work, but the large frontline base and substantial existing ticketing automation keep the remaining labor surface modest.

d5.bridge: AMC reports 2025 North American box office of $8.9 billion versus $8.7 billion in 2024 and $11.4 billion in 2019, while Marcus reports calendar-2025 comparable attendance down 4.6%. Cinema United reports that 77% of Americans aged 12-74 visited a theater in the prior year, rising habitual and Gen Z frequency, and 115 wide releases projected for 2026 versus 111 in 2025. I translate these mixed revenue, attendance, consumer-survey, and film-supply indicators into a roughly stable five-year constant-price, constant-quality quantity path, discounting nominal ticket inflation, trade-group optimism, film-slate volatility, shorter windows, streaming substitution, economic cycles, and continued site closures. Stronger release supply and younger attendance could raise quantity; the still-incomplete post-2019 recovery and 2025 attendance weakness could lower it.
caveats: Box office is nominal revenue, not constant-price quantity.
Public-chain attendance and trade-group surveys may not represent LMM independent theaters.
Demand depends unusually heavily on distributor release schedules and a small number of titles.
d5.change_evidence: Comscore or Cinema United longitudinal admissions by indoor theater type and market, deflated ticket revenue, screen openings and closures, release counts, theatrical-window length, and cohort frequency through several film slates would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: The theatrical habit remains widespread and film supply is improving, but admissions are volatile and have not regained the pre-pandemic pattern. A stable central path with a wide downside and upside range is more defensible than extrapolating either one weak year or promotional consumer research.

caveats: No public source measures eligibility within the supplied LMM-band firm count.
The supplied n uses a general Information Services EBITDA margin that may not fit film-rental, concession, and lease economics.
A financially active location can be part of a larger chain rather than a separately transferable firm.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level 512131 census joining screens, operating status, recurring admissions and concession revenue, normalized cash flow, lease control, film-booking arrangements, related entities, and standalone transferability would materially change the share.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is centered on operating theaters and exhibiting films, which normally provides a repeated external-customer service. Discounts cover film-festival or itinerant exhibitors without durable operations, dormant or distressed venues, non-operating property entities, captive screens, weak standalone financials, and theaters whose film, lease, franchise, or management arrangements prevent a transferable control position.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 512131 ($2,418,895,818) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 512131 ($12,810,730,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1728 (margins.json[51]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Information Services EBITDA/Sales 17.28% (conservative sector default)); revenue/employee $117,394 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 512131); class <5: 556 firms, avg revenue/firm $421,523 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $72,839 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 369 firms, avg revenue/firm $554,791 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $95,868 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 400 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,028,365 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $177,701 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 392 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,273,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $565,661 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 68 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,400,426 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,697,994 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $329,352,071 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $56,912,038 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 68

o.bridge: Cinema United's consumer evidence links theater choice to large-format screens, upgraded seating, concessions, in-seat ordering, and an immersive experience, while the Census definition centers on operating a theater and exhibiting films. I map continuing year-five demand for the current theatrical-service basket to a high operator-required share because a public venue, projection, safety, rights compliance, food service, and guest accountability cannot be supplied entirely by software. I discount portions shifting to home streaming, self-service ticketing and ordering, pop-up or venue-rental formats, and services eliminated rather than operated. Trade-group evidence likely overstates attachment to premium venues; volume loss to streaming is primarily in d5, so I avoid applying the full substitution risk twice.
caveats: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required share.
The trade association has an advocacy interest in emphasizing experiential durability.
Self-service changes labor intensity without necessarily removing the theater operator.
o.change_evidence: Consumer and venue-level data separating admissions lost to home viewing from transactions self-served within theaters, plus operating models for autonomous venues, third-party pop-ups, safety, projection rights, and food service, would materially change the share.
o.rationale: Software can self-serve transactions and reduce labor, but it cannot deliver the physical shared exhibition experience without an accountable venue operator. Most surviving theatrical quantity therefore remains operator-required.

q.bridge: Marcus reports a 3.7% increase in average ticket price and a 4.1% increase in concession revenue per person in fiscal 2025, while also using strategic discounts and technology to improve labor productivity and sales. I infer that savings in theater-owned labor and overhead are not contractually passed through to patrons, then discount distributor film-rental participation in admissions, consumer price sensitivity, loyalty discounts, competing theaters and streaming, vendor fees, reinvestment in premium formats, and renewal repricing of leases and software. A regional public chain's pricing tools and scale likely overstate retention for independents, while local catchments and fixed posted prices can preserve initial savings.
caveats: Price increases during inflation do not directly measure retention of AI-enabled savings.
Film-rental terms vary by title and may capture part of admissions economics.
Independent theaters may reinvest savings to remain competitive rather than retain them as cash flow.
q.change_evidence: Theater-level cohorts linking workflow automation to labor hours, ticket and concession prices, film rent, technology fees, attendance, promotions, service levels, and operating margin over five years would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Admissions and concessions are posted-price consumer sales rather than cost-plus contracts, so labor efficiencies can initially remain with the operator. Competitive promotions, distributor economics, technology cost, and the need to reinvest in the experience prevent full five-year retention.

rho.bridge: Marcus reports computerized box offices, self-service ticket kiosks, online and app ticketing, mobile and QR food ordering, digital menu systems, and plans to use technology to improve labor productivity. I translate that demonstrated digital workflow base into five-year implementation of the narrower remaining exposed tasks, then discount integration cost, legacy POS and loyalty data, thin management teams, customer-service exceptions, food-safety and cash controls, variable film slates, and the need for on-site human coverage. A public regional chain likely overstates systems maturity and capital access for LMM independents, while packaged exhibitor software could narrow the gap.
caveats: Observed digitization is not evidence that AI has delivered equivalent labor removal.
Marcus is larger and better capitalized than the screened LMM population.
Some remaining exposed tasks occur only during peaks, limiting removable staffing.
rho.change_evidence: A representative survey of independent 512131 theaters documenting production AI by workflow, integration and vendor costs, exception rates, human review, verified hours saved, and adoption by screen and revenue size would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Ticketing, scheduling, loyalty, marketing, inventory, ordering, and back-office systems provide practical integration points. Implementation is less plausible for the physical work dominating employment and may be constrained by vendors and limited independent-theater capital.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell documents completed nightclub and theater business sales from 2021 through 2025 and reports that, across its broader small-business marketplace, 30% of owners planning to sell cited retirement and 49% of brokers said Baby Boomers already made up most listings. I translate the existence of a theater sale market and broad seller pressure into a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability, then discount the absence of an exact-512131 denominator, the benchmark's inclusion of live theaters and nightclubs, chain-owned sites, lease and film-booking constraints, distressed closures, and property sales without a continuing theater operation. Marketplace listings overrepresent owner-operated sale candidates, so the base is below a naive extrapolation from seller sentiment.
caveats: BizBuySell's theater benchmark includes nightclubs, live-performance venues, and smaller businesses outside the supplied EBITDA band.
The seller survey has no exact-industry denominator and measures intentions, not completed transfers.
Theater closures and property dispositions do not qualify when the operating service does not survive.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of 512131 business sales, mergers, lease assignments, chain acquisitions, closures, and asset-only real-estate transactions, matched to the eligible-firm denominator and surviving operating control, would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Independent theaters can be sold as operating businesses, and owner retirement creates a plausible pipeline, but many screen changes are chain portfolio transactions, lease terminations, or closures rather than qualifying LMM control transfers.

principal_driver: Digitized ticketing, loyalty, marketing, scheduling, and back-office systems create implementable efficiency around repeat admissions and concession revenue.
principal_weakness: Most labor is physical and demand depends on volatile film supply while the buyer-accessible LMM operator pool is small.

sources.supports: Exact 512131 scope as operating non-drive-in motion-picture theaters and exhibiting films or videos at film festivals.
sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual

sources.supports: Combined 512130 occupation employment shares and wages for management, food service, ushers and ticket takers, sales, administration, cleaning, security, and maintenance.
sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Motion Picture and Video Exhibition

sources.supports: Theater ticketing and food-ordering technology, self-service systems, labor-productivity intent, attendance, ticket and concession pricing, alternate content, and operating risks.
sources.title: The Marcus Corporation 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: North American 2025 box office of $8.9 billion compared with $8.7 billion in 2024 and $11.4 billion in 2019, plus exhibitor operating responses.
sources.title: AMC Entertainment Holdings 2025 Form 10-K/A

sources.supports: Consumer moviegoing incidence and frequency, theater reinvestment, release-slate indicators, and experiential attributes valued by moviegoers.
sources.title: Strength of Theatrical Exhibition: December 2025 Update

sources.supports: Broad small-business sale activity, seller motives including retirement, and broker evidence on Baby Boomer listings.
sources.title: BizBuySell 2025 Fourth Quarter Insight Report

sources.supports: Observed sales and financial benchmarks for nightclub and theater businesses reported to BizBuySell from 2021 through 2025.
sources.title: Nightclub and Theater Business Valuation Benchmarks

title: Motion Picture Theaters (except Drive-Ins)
